question.text: Which of the following is a key difference between an open-ended mutual fund scheme and a close-ended mutual fund scheme?

answer.text: Open-ended schemes have a fixed NAV while close-ended schemes have a floating NAV
Open-ended schemes allow purchase and redemption at any time while close-ended schemes have a fixed maturity period
Open-ended schemes invest only in equity while close-ended schemes invest only in debt
Open-ended schemes are regulated by RBI while close-ended schemes are regulated by SEBI

explanation.text: **The Logic:** An open-ended scheme offers investors an option to buy units from the fund at any time and sell units back to the fund at any time, with no fixed maturity period. Close-ended funds sell a specific number of units with a fixed maturity, and units can be traded on stock exchanges. **The Trap:** Both open and close-ended schemes have NAVs that change based on portfolio values. Both can invest in equity or debt. Both are regulated by SEBI. **Cross-Exam Context:** Mutual fund scheme classification is extensively tested in NISM Series V-A and V-B (Mutual Fund Distributors and Foundation). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** For clients needing liquidity, open-ended schemes are preferable. Close-ended schemes suit investors with a defined time horizon who don't need interim liquidity.
